coredata.dc:title: In situ visualization of apoptosis process and modulation of cancer cell ablation based on a monocomponent multi-functional smart nanozyme
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Shuo
Xiao, Yue
Sun, Yuanqiang
Qu, Lingbo
Cai, Qiyong
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: Nanozymes have exhibited an excellent prospect in cancer ablation. However, the real-time sensing of anticancer efficacy in catalytic process is often neglected. Designing the smart nanozymes for in situ visualizing treatment efficacy at subcellular level during catalytic therapy is crucial for precise cancer treatment, but hampered owing to the lack of a robust design strategy and efficient synthesis method. Herein, a monocomponent multi-functional photo-responsive carbon dots-based (CDs) nanozyme for instant self-predicting therapy efficacy and synchronous cancer cell ablation is developed through a direct microwave-assisted method. The CDs nanozyme can significantly induce cancer cell ablation due to its unique dual enzyme-mimic activities (oxidase-mimic and peroxidase-mimic), mitochondria-targeting ability, light-response feature improved synergistic therapy and high spatiotemporal selection. More importantly, the excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated from catalytic treatment induces mitochondrial oxidative stress, accompanied by decreased mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP), which results in the transfer of CDs nanozyme from the mitochondrion to the nucleolus and thus enables the in situ real-time detection of therapy efficiency via fluorescent signal migration. This work not only represents an emerging paradigm with the self-predicting function for precise cancer treatment but also inspires the future design of the new generation of nanozyme.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanozyme
Treatment efficacy self-sensing
Theranostic agent
Photo-responsive

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The oxidase-like activity of CDs nanozyme
The peroxidase-like activity of CDs nanozyme
Synthesis and characterization of CDs nanozyme
Dual enzyme-mimic activities of CDs nanozyme
Differentiation of live and dead cells
Simultaneous catalytic cancer cell ablation and self-predicting treatment efficacy

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Joint Fund for Science and Technology of Henan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [ 21974125 , 22274143 ], Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in the University of Henan Province ( 22TRTSTHN002 ), the 111 Project [ D20003 , CXJD2021001 ], and the Key Projects of the Joint Fund for Science and Technology of Henan Province ( 222301420007 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of CDs nanozyme. (A) Synthesis route of CDs nanozyme. (B) TEM image of CDs nanozyme (inset: lattice fringes of CDs nanozyme shown by HRTEM image). (C) FTIR pattern, (D) XPS spectrum, and (E) zeta potential of CDs nanozyme.

Optical properties of CDs nanozyme. (A) The UV–vis absorption and (B) fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs nanozyme (10 μg/mL). (C) Excitation-independent fluorescent emission and (D) photostability of CDs nanozyme. (E) The effect of biological species (the concentration of metal ions and amino acids was 1 mM) on the fluorescence signals of CDs nanozyme (1. CDs nanozyme, 2. K+, 3. Mg2+, 4. Zn2+, 5. Ca2+, 6. Cu2+, 7. Na+, 8. Ba2+, 9. Fe2+, 10. Arg, 11. Ser, 12. Lys, 13. Thr, 14. His, 15. Met, 16, Pro, 17, Asp, 18, Leu, 19, Gln, 20. Val, 21. Tyr. (F) The fluorescence intensity of CDs nanozyme at different pH conditions. (G) The fluorescence spectra of CDs nanozyme with the addition of RNA and (H) DNA.

OXD-mimic activity of CDs nanozyme. (A) Schematic illustration of CDs nanozyme's OXD-like enzyme-mimic activity. (B) The absorption spectra of TMB catalyzed by CDs nanozyme with light at different time. (C) Time-dependent absorbance (652 nm) of TMB-based mixtures with different catalytic conditions (red line: TMB/CDs nanozyme under light, green line: TMB/CDs nanozyme in dark, blue line: TMB under light). (D) Reaction kinetics, and (E) double-reciprocal plots for OXD-like activity of CDs nanozyme. (F) Assessment of effects of varied scavengers on ROS production catalyzed by OXD-like activity of CDs nanozyme.

Peroxidase-mimic activities of CDs nanozyme. (A) Schematic illustration of CDs nanozyme's POD-like enzyme-mimic activity. (B) The absorption spectra of TMB catalyzed by CDs nanozyme with light in the presence of H2O2. (C) Time-dependent absorption intensity at 652 nm in various reaction systems (red line: TMB/CDs nanozyme/ H2O2 with light, green line: TMB/CDs nanozyme/H2O2 in dark, blue line: TMB/H2O2 with light, yellow line: TMB/CDs nanozyme with light). (D-G) Michaelis-Menten curves and double-reciprocal plots for POD-like activity of CDs nanozyme with different substrates. (H) Influence of varied scavengers on ROS generation catalyzed by POD-like activity of CDs nanozyme.

Differentiation of live and dead cells by CDs nanozyme. (A) Confocal images of CDs nanozyme-pretreated different cell lines co-stained with MTDR. (B) Confocal images of living HeLa cells co-stained with CDs nanozyme and MTDR and fixed HeLa cells labeled with CDs nanozyme and PI. Scale bar: 15 µm.

Cancer cell ablation induced by CDs nanozyme. (A) Schematic presentation of monitoring intracellular ROS. (B) Time-lapse confocal images of irradiated HeLa cells treated with CDs nanozyme and H2DCF-DA. Scale bar: 25 µm. (C) Fluorescence confocal images of dead and living cells co-stained with PI and Calcein-AM. Scale bar: 100 µm.

Self-predicting treatment efficacy during catalytic therapy by CDs nanozyme. (A) Schematic representation of the multi-functions of CDs nanozyme for simultaneous photo-induced catalytic therapy and self-reporting treatment efficacy. (B) Flow cytometry analysis of HeLa cells cultured with PI and Annexin V-FITC after incubation with CDs nanozyme under various irradiation times. (C) Real-time visualization of HeLa cells under continuous laser exposure labeled with CDs nanozyme and NTDR. Scale bar: 25 µm.
Illustration of in situ visualization of apoptosis process and synchronous light-triggered cancer cell ablation by using CDs nanozyme.

head.ce:title: In situ visualization of apoptosis process and modulation of cancer cell ablation based on a monocomponent multi-functional smart nanozyme

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterial and Medical Theranostic, Zhengzhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanozymes have exhibited an excellent prospect in cancer ablation. However, the real-time sensing of anticancer efficacy in catalytic process is often neglected. Designing the smart nanozymes for in situ visualizing treatment efficacy at subcellular level during catalytic therapy is crucial for precise cancer treatment, but hampered owing to the lack of a robust design strategy and efficient synthesis method. Herein, a monocomponent multi-functional photo-responsive carbon dots-based (CDs) nanozyme for instant self-predicting therapy efficacy and synchronous cancer cell ablation is developed through a direct microwave-assisted method. The CDs nanozyme can significantly induce cancer cell ablation due to its unique dual enzyme-mimic activities (oxidase-mimic and peroxidase-mimic), mitochondria-targeting ability, light-response feature improved synergistic therapy and high spatiotemporal selection. More importantly, the excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated from catalytic treatment induces mitochondrial oxidative stress, accompanied by decreased mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP), which results in the transfer of CDs nanozyme from the mitochondrion to the nucleolus and thus enables the in situ real-time detection of therapy efficiency via fluorescent signal migration. This work not only represents an emerging paradigm with the self-predicting function for precise cancer treatment but also inspires the future design of the new generation of nanozyme.

Monocomponent multi-functional smart carbon dots-based nanozyme was designed for synchronous instant in situ self-sensing of treatment efficacy and dual enzyme-like catalytic cancer cell ablation.
ga1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The Supporting Information is available free of charge.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes, the nanomaterials with enzyme-like activities, have been applied in broad research fields including catalysis, sensing, and disease therapy [1–4]. For example, Yan et al. synthesized a carbon dot nanozyme and deciphered its catalytic mechanism. The neuron cells in ischemic stroke mice are effectively protected by treating with the nanozyme [5]. Specifically, Nanozymes have drawn keen interest in cancer ablation owing to their tunable enzyme-like properties, high robustness against harsh environments and specific toxic [6,7]. For instance, Shi et al. prepared the inorganic biomimetic nanozyme by co-loading Au and Fe3O4 on dendritic mesoporous silica nanoparticles. The nanozyme could lead to cancer cell death through triggering the cascade reactions in especial tumor microenvironment [8]. Qu’s group constructed the MnO2 @PtCo nanoﬂowers by the self-assembly of nanozymes. The nanoﬂowers can not only promote cell apoptosis through ROS generation but also alleviate hypoxia [9]. Liu et al. manufactured the nano-agent, named Ce6 @MnO2-PEG, by blending chlorine e6 (Ce6), polyethylene glycol (PEG) and MnO2. Cancer cells were killed efficiently by the nanoplatform through regulating tumor microenvironment [10]. Although nanozymes have made tremendous advance for cancer treatment, some drawbacks are still existed. Firstly, most nanozymes agents have complex synthetic routes, limited reproducibility, and high side effects, which dramatically hinder their application in cancer ablation [11]. Secondly, most reported nanozymes emphasize synergistic treatment, while the instant responses of anticancer efficacy are usually neglected [12,13]. In fact, due to cancer heterogeneity, various responses are observed in disparate treatment methods. Real-time and in situ therapeutic effect evaluation is beneficial for avoiding drug resistance from excessive treatments and cancer aggravation from ineffective therapeutics, further realizing precision therapy. To obtain the information of anticancer efficiency, further tests, such as microplate reader detection and flow cytometry analysis, are usually acquired, which is time-consuming, delayed, ex situ and costly [14,15]. Based on the above issues, it is exceedingly desirable to design simple nanozymes for effective therapy and real-time self-reporting of treatment assessment.

Due to the advantages of facile preparation, low cost, and excellent fluorescence properties, carbon dots have been applied in broad research fields including catalysis, sensing, and disease diagnosis [16]. In recent years, a few CDs nanozymes have been reported as artificial enzymes for cancer treatment owing to their brilliant intrinsic catalytic properties [17,18]. Meanwhile, some CDs were reported as ﬂuorescent probes for apoptosis sensing, which could provide valuable information for therapy effect. Considering the enzyme-mimic activities and monitoring apoptosis characteristics were realized in separate CDs, it is possible to realize real-time assessment of catalytic efficacy by combining its optical characteristics and nanozyme properties, which is yet to be reported. Particularly, photo-responsive nanozyme is activated with light to produce ROS for promoting cell death. As an emerging therapy modality, photo-responsive nanozyme has aroused widespread attention due to its predominant advantages, such as high spatiotemporal selection, noninvasiveness, and minor drug resistance [19]. Therefore, the development of photo-responsive CDs nanozyme, allowing for simultaneous light-driven therapy and real-time evaluation of treatment outcomes, represents a significant but challenging breakthrough.

Herein in this study, we designed the monocomponent multi-functional CDs nanozyme for real-time self-reporting treatment efficacy during the catalytic process ( Scheme 1). The CDs nanozyme with dual enzyme-mimic activities was prepared using a one-step microwave-assisted strategy. Possessing the mimicking activities of oxidase and peroxidase, the CDs nanozyme could efficiently guide cell ablation through ROS formation upon light exposure. In addition, the mitochondrion-specific feature of CDs nanozyme is sufficiently employed to improve the therapeutic effect because the intracellular location of photo-response agents can severely aﬀect the therapy efficacy due to ROS's short diffusion distance and lifetime [20]. In particular, the concomitant translocation of CDs nanozyme from the mitochondrion to the nucleolus during catalytic treatment enabled the construction of a self-reporting system to monitor the therapy process in real-time. Real-time feedback of diagnosis processes is important for determining the exact treatment end point, which is helpful for controlling the CDs nanozyme dose to avoid excessive/under treatment and minimize side eﬀect. Moreover, the strategy of self-reporting treatment efficacy is time-saving and low-cost compared with conventional diagnosis method. Overall, this work provides a light-triggered CDs nanozyme for sensing the process of photocatalytic therapy, which makes the realization of exact treatment possible. In addition, the result will facilitate the development of multi-functional nanozymes for cancer theranostics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The oxidase-like activity of CDs nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: The mixtures of CDs nanozyme and 3, 3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were irradiated with white light (400–780 nm, 29.6 mW/cm2). The absorption spectra of TMB were recorded by UV–vis spectrophotometer. Increased amounts (0.13–2.67 mM) of TMB solution were prepared to explore the kinetic curves of CDs nanozyme.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The peroxidase-like activity of CDs nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: For the peroxidase (POD)-like activity testing of CDs nanozyme, the absorbance of HAc-NaAc buffer dispersions containing CDs nanozyme, TMB, and H2O2 at 652 nm were measured with regular irradiation intervals. For kinetic investigation, assays were implemented in HAc-NaAc buffer, including CDs nanozyme, TMB, and different amounts of H2O2 (20–200 μM) or containing CDs nanozyme, H2O2, and progressively concentrations of TMB (20–300 μM).

HeLa cells were stained with CDs nanozyme (10 μg/mL) for 30 min, followed by MitoTracker™ Deep Red (MTDR, 50 nM) for 20 min. Then, the cells were imaged by TCS SP8 X confocal microscope. In addition, the co-localization experiments of HepG-2 cells, HT-1080 cells, and Huh-7 cells were carried out with the same condition. Similarly, the co-staining tests (CDs nanozyme and LysoTracker™ Deep Red, CDs nanozyme and NucRed Live 647) were executed in HeLa cells. The incubation time of LysoTracker Green DND-26 (LTG, 50 nM) and NucRed Live 647 (NRL, two drops per mL) was 20 min. Besides, the co-localization studies of CDs nanozyme and propidium iodide (PI) were employed in fixed cells pretreated with 4% paraformaldehyde. The proper excitation filter was selected for each dye: CDs nanozyme (405 nm), MTDR and NRL (638 nm), LTG (490 nm), and PI (535 nm). Emission: 480–600 nm for CDs nanozyme, 655–725 nm for MTDR and NRL, 500 − 530 nm for LTG, 590 − 650 nm for PI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: In this contribution, CDs nanozyme was synthesized by the microwave-assisted strategy with 1-phenylpyrrolidine and citric acid as the precursors ( Fig. 1A). To determine the morphological characteristics of CDs nanozyme, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded. As shown in Fig. 1B, the shape of CDs nanozyme was regular, and its diameter distribution is between 2.0 and 3.6 nm, with an average size of 2.8 nm (Fig. S1). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image demonstrated that the lattice fringe spacing of CDs nanozyme is 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the (100) plane of graphene [21,22]. To further analyze the chemical structure of CDs nanozyme, Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured. As displayed in Fig. 1C, the absorption bands at the wavenumbers of 1635, 2925, and 3397 cm–1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO, C-H, and O-H, respectively. The peaks at 1516 and 1129 cm–1 were assigned to the CC and C-O stretching frequencies [23]. XPS spectrum showed that the CDs nanozyme is composed of C, N, and O elements, with 80.84%, 4.47%, and 14.69%, respectively (Fig. 1D). The C 1 s spectra can be classified into two peaks at 284.6 and 285.4 eV which are ascribed to C-C/CC and C-N/C-O (Fig. S2). The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum reveals two distinct peaks at 399.5 eV and 400.6 eV, which are deriving from pyridinic N and pyrrolic N [24]. The deconvoluted O 1 s spectra shows peaks at 531.4 eV (CO) and 532.4 eV (C-O) [25]. The results of XPS spectra further verifies the chemical compositions of CDs nanozyme and validates the analysis of FTIR. Under the high temperature produced by microwave, Friedel-Crafts reaction executes between 1-phenylpyrrolidine and citric acid, ammonium cations are generated on the surface of CDs nanozyme. As expected, the zeta potential of CDs nanozyme is + 34.12 mV, suggesting the cation groups on the CDs nanozyme (Fig. 1E).

After a detailed structural characterization of CDs nanozyme, its optical properties were surveyed. As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, the UV−vis absorption spectrum of CDs nanozyme shows two distinct absorption peaks at 370 nm and 314 nm, corresponding to n–π * (surface chemical groups) and π-π * (carbon skeleton structure) transition [26]. Furthermore, the optimal emission of CDs nanozyme located at 532 nm (Fig. 2B), and the fluorescence excitation region matches well with the absorption peak of 370 nm, demonstrating that band at 370 nm contributes to the emission of CDs nanozyme [27]. Notably, the large Stokes shifts (162 nm) can reduce excitation backscattering to avoid self-absorption, improve the signal-to-noise ratio, and ultimately realize precise imaging. In addition, the emission peak shifts negligibly when the excitation wavelength varied from 360 nm to 390 nm, indicating the excitation-independent fluorescence of CDs nanozyme (Fig. 2 C). The result also suggests that the fluorescence of CDs nanozyme originates from surface chemical defects [28,29].

Furthermore, no obvious photobleaching of CDs nanozyme is observed when it was continuously exposed to laser radiation at 370 nm for 2 h, verifying the excellent photostability of CDs nanozyme (Fig. 2D). Particularly, the fluorescence spectra of CDs nanozyme changes slightly in the presence of various biological constituents, such as amino acids and metal ions (Fig. 2E, S3, S4). The fluorescence intensity of CDs nanozyme is not influenced with the pH changes from 4.0 to 9.0, indicating the fluorescent signals of CDs nanozyme could remain stable in physiological environments (Fig. 2 F, S5). These results also exhibit the advantages of CDs nanozyme for the application of cell imaging [30]. On the contrary, when the nucleic acid responsiveness of CDs nanozyme was evaluated, a gradually elevated fluorescence of CDs nanozyme was noticed with the addition of RNA or DNA, indicating the excellent aﬃnity of CDs nanozyme and nucleic acids (Fig. 2 G, 2 H, S6, S7). The exciting results verify that the CDs nanozyme with cationic functional groups could incorporate into negatively charged nucleic acids. Intercalative binding is one of the binding modes between nanoparticles and nucleic acids. The nanoparticles with planar heteroaromatic ring or aromatic systems can insert into the base pairs of nucleic acids through intercalative interactions. When nanoparticles are inserted into nucleic acids base, the electrons in the excited state of them favor the radiative transition in the returning of ground state. Thus, the ﬂuorescence emission is enhanced. [31]. Furthermore, the motions of superficial groups on CDs nanozyme are restricted, and nonradiative decay is hindered. Finally, the emission of CDs nanozyme is activated [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual enzyme-mimic activities of CDs nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para:
According to the reported literatures, pyrrolic N is the catalytic active site of oxidase (OXD)-mimic CDs nanozyme [33]. The content of pyridinic N has a positive correlation with POD-like activity in carbon nanostructures [34,35]. Considering the pyrrolic N and pyridinic N structures in CDs nanozyme, its dual enzyme-mimic activities would be detected. To test the enzyme-like activities of CDs nanozyme ( Fig. 3 A), TMB was employed as the substrates, which could be oxidized into TMBox with a characteristic absorption peak at around 652 nm. The OXD-like activity of CDs nanozyme, which introduced dioxygen (O2) as an acceptor to produce water (H2O), was investigated with TMB as the hydrogen (H) donor [17]. The OXD-like activity of CDs nanozyme was investigated under different pH and temperature (Fig. S8). Then, the reaction time of TMB-based mixture was explored, as exhibited in Fig. 3B, with extension of reaction time, the absorption peak at 652 nm of TMB remarkably increases in the presence of CDs nanozyme under light irradiation. As a control group, the solution of TMB is irradiated with the same light intensity, the absorbance of TMB keeps unchanged with reaction time, indicating that the sole light could not realize the oxidation of TMB (Fig. S9). Similarly, no obvious absorption signal of TMB is observed with CDs nanozyme in dark, confirming the potential of CDs nanozyme as a white light-driven OXD mimic enzyme (Fig. 3 C). Besides, the steady-state kinetic experiments were explored to assess the catalytic activity of CDs nanozyme, which typically followed Michaelis–Menten curves (Fig. 3D, S10). The value of Michaelis–Menten constant (K
M ) and Vmax are 498.85 μM and 6.67 * 10−9 Ms−1 (Fig. 3E), suggesting good catalytic efﬁciency of CDs nanozyme as an OXD nanozyme. The specific activity (SA) of CDs nanozyme is 0.697 U/mg (Fig. S11). In addition, the catalytic capacity of CDs nanozyme is dramatically inhibited in the presence of N2, demonstrating the assistance of O2 in TMB oxidation (Fig. S12). Moreover, ascorbic acid, isopropanol, tryptophan, tertiary butanol, and EDTA-2Na were employed as superoxide anion (·O2 −), electron, singlet oxygen, hydroxyl radical, and hole scavengers, respectively (Fig. 3 F, S13) [33]. It is found that the oxidation of TMB decreases signiﬁcantly in the presence of ascorbic acid or EDTA-2Na, confirming that the hole and·O2 − accelerate the oxidation reaction. At the same time, no obvious eﬀect is observed with the addition of other scavengers. In addition, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrum is recorded to unambiguously identify ROS. 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) is employed for trapping·O2 − [9]. As shown in Fig. S14, the characteristic signal of·O2 − is generated in the mixture of CDs nanozyme under light. On the contrary, no obvious signal appears in the control group. The results validate that the CDs nanozyme has indeed the potential to produce·O2 −.

We next investigated the peroxidase (POD)-like activity of CDs nanozyme, which oxidated substrate accompanied by the reduction of H2O2 in the catalytic reaction ( Fig. 4 A) [11]. The reaction conditions of CDs nanozyme was optimized with changed temperature and pH (Fig. S15). After that, the reaction time of TMB system was studied. As depicted in Fig. 4B, the absorbance of TMB prominently increases in the presence of H2O2 under light-irradiated CDs nanozyme system in a time-dependent manner. In contrast, the increasing absorption peak of TMB do not occur in control groups, such as the reaction system of TMB/CDs nanozyme/H2O2 in dark, TMB/ H2O2 with light, and TMB/CDs nanozyme with light (Fig. 4 C, S16). These results demonstrate the inferior POD-like activity of CDs nanozyme activity without light illumination. The enzyme kinetic experiments show that the K
M is 172.42 μM for TMB substrate and 86.52 μM for H2O2 substrate, indicating the high catalytic efﬁciency of CDs nanozyme as a photo-responsive peroxidase mimic (Fig. 4D-G, S17, S18). The POD-like SA of CDs nanozyme reaches to 2.185 U/mg (Fig. S19). Besides, reactive species capture assays prove that the addition of EDTA and ascorbic acid significantly impede the POD-like activity of CDs nanozyme, manifesting that the holes can facilitate the transfer of electrons and·O2 − appears during the catalytic reaction (Fig. 4H, S20). ESR spectrum is also conducted. As exhibited in Fig. S21, the specific signal of·O2 − is obtained in the CDs nanozyme/H2O2 system in the dark and light. The results are consistent with the analysis of Fig. 4 C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differentiation of live and dead cells
ce:section.ce:para: The exceptional emission properties and unique enzyme-catalytic activities of the CDs nanozyme encouraged us to explore its bio-application. It was reported that mitochondrion in live cells has a negative MMP value (about −180 mV) [36,37]. Because of cations on CDs nanozyme, we hypothesize that it will accumulate in the mitochondrion by driving of electrostatic attraction. Meanwhile, based on the combination with nucleic acids, mitochondrion-to-nucleolus redistribution of CDs nanozyme would appear when the MMP is depolarized in dead cells. Consequently, the live and dead cells can be differentiated by CDs nanozyme's changed subcellular organelle distribution. For conﬁrming the speculation, the co-staining trials were studied using CDs nanozyme and the commercial mitochondria-speciﬁc dye (MitoTracker™ Deep Red, MTDR) in HepG-2 cells. As depicted in Fig. 5A, confocal images of HepG-2 cells labeled with CDs nanozyme display filamentous shapes. Intracellular staining, which co-localized with MTDR, shows a high Pearson's correlation coeﬃcient of 0.83, demonstrating a good overlap between the two variables, also identifying the good mitochondrion-anchoring ability of CDs nanozyme. Additionally, localization of CDs nanozyme and MTDR in mitochondrion is generalizable in other cancer cell lines, such as HT-1080 cells, Huh-7 cells, and HeLa cells, indicating the universal mitochondrial-targeted capability of CDs nanozyme. Except for cancer cell lines, the distribution of CDs nanozyme in normal cells was also explored. As displayed in Fig. S22, the Pearson's correlation coeﬃcient reaches to 0.81 and 0.82 in hek293 cells and HL-7702 cells, confirming the specific mitochondrion-staining capability of CDs nanozyme. Subsequently, co-localization experiments with a commercial lysosome probe (LysoTracker Green DND-26, LTG) were performed to identify the mitochondrion-speciﬁcity of CDs nanozyme. Fig. S23 illustrated that the ﬂuorescence from LTG partly merges from CDs nanozyme, and low Pearson's correlation coeﬃcients (Rr=0.32) is acquired between LTG and CDs nanozyme. The results fully affirm the high selectivity of CDs nanozyme for mitochondrion. To mimic the dead cells, 4% paraformaldehyde is employed to fix the cells. As exhibited in Fig. 5B, the green ﬂuorescence of CDs nanozyme appears in the nucleolus. PI is the commercial dye to stain nucleus in fixed cells. The fluorescent signals of CDs nanozyme merges well with the red ﬂuorescence from PI. Significantly, the Pearson's correlation coeﬃcients reach to 0.82, confirming most CDs nanozyme located in the nucleolus. The above results reveal that the CDs nanozyme can visualize live and dead cells through varying spatial distribution.

The photo-responsive catalytic activity of CDs nanozyme to induce cancer cell apoptosis was further investigated. Firstly, the 2′,7′-dichloroﬂuorescein diacetate (H2DCF-DA), a cell-permeable ROS indicator, was employed to explore intracellular ROS generation ( Fig. 6A) [38]. As illustrated in Fig. 6B, HeLa cells treated with CDs nanozyme upon white-light irradiation show apparent green fluorescence. In contrast, almost no fluorescence appears in HeLa cells incubated with CDs nanozyme in dark, and irradiated with white light in the absence of CDs nanozyme. This observation validates that intracellular ROS was remarkably produced with CDs nanozyme under white-light illumination. Moreover, stronger dichloroﬂuorescein (DCF) fluorescence is acquired with increasing exposure time, meaning more ROS is produced, which is beneﬁcial for inducing mitochondrion damage and further triggering cell death.

Next, the Calcein-AM and PI staining assay was carried out to confirm the photocatalytic killing performance of CDs nanozyme. Calcein-AM can easily penetrate the plasma membrane. The green fluorescence of the probe is triggered after the hydrolyzation reaction with the endogenous esterase in living cells. As indicated in Fig. 6C, HeLa cells treated with PBS with light or CDs nanozyme in the dark are still maintained alive, as showed by the green emission of Calcein-AM, suggesting the low cytotoxicity of CDs nanozyme and inefficient light source destruction. In marked contrast, homogeneous red emission from PI appears in the cells cultured with CDs nanozyme under light, affirming most cells are killed. These results demonstrate that the CDs nanozyme can be applied as a promising photo-responsive nanozyme agent in cancer cell ablation. Moreover, a CCK-8 assay was executed to estimate the cell-killing eﬀect of CDs nanozyme. As indicated in Fig. S24, the cell viability of CDs nanozyme without white light irradiation is more than 85% (10 μg/mL), demonstrating that CDs nanozyme has acceptable cytotoxicity in the dark. In contrast, when illuminated with white light for 10 min, the cell viability reduces to only 23%, proving the high phototoxicity of CDs nanozyme.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous catalytic cancer cell ablation and self-predicting treatment efficacy
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the fact that the CDs nanozyme possess the ability of mitochondrial targeting and light-induced ROS production, which elicits mitochondrial oxidative stress to induce cell death [39,40]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence signals of CDs nanozyme in nucleolus enhances evidently within dead cells due to the combination with nucleic acids. Thus, we speculate that increased cell apoptosis would appear with extended illumination time, the CDs nanozyme would break away from the initial mitochondrion, and the nucleolus would be lighted up ( Fig. 7 A). Flow cytometric staining experiments were examined to quantify cell apoptosis. Annexin V-FITC detects early apoptotic cells by binding with phosphatidylserine, and PI emits red fluorescence when combined with DNA in necrosis or late apoptosis cells [41]. The results of Fig. 7B showed that, without white light irradiation, there is unobvious apoptosis in the presence of CDs nanozyme. In contrast, both ﬂuorescence signals of Annexin-V FITC and PI are gradually enhanced with increased illumination time. With lighting time from 0 to 12 min, the early apoptosis rises from 3.97% to 29.8%, and the late apoptosis increases from 0.60% to 54.8%. On the contrary, insignificant cell apoptosis occurs in respective control cells incubated with PBS. It is worth mentioning that another 24 h is needed to obtain the treatment effect results after illumination, and additional reagents are added to the system, making the strategy costly, complicated, and time-consuming. In contrast, the prepared CDs nanozyme is employed to monitor the treatment effect in real-time. Fig. 7 C shows the NRL (NucRed Live 647, commercial nucleus probe) performed co-localization experiments with CDs nanozyme. Pearson's correlation coeﬃcients increases from 0.27 to 0.59 after continuous laser exposure, verifying that the CDs nanozyme detaches from mitochondrion and transfers into nucleolus with ROS-activated cell apoptosis. The results reveals that the CDs nanozyme can realize both light-activated ROS release and in situ real-time sensing of catalytic-therapy efficacy based on location transfer. In addition, the excellent interrelation between fluorescent signals translocation from the mitochondrion to the nucleolus of CDs nanozyme and the degree of enzyme-catalytic treatment activated cell death is acquired according to the analysis of flow cytometry results and confocal images. These results confirm that the location transferring of CDs nanozyme is related to light-triggered catalytic treatment. Therefore, the CDs nanozyme could monitor the cellular death in real-time, which is precisely caused by itself under white light irradiation. The system is low-cost, simple, and will facilitate the development of cancer theranostics.

Based on the above studies, the mechanism of mitochondrion-to-nucleolus migration of CDs nanozyme is proposed. Initially, CDs nanozyme is distributed in mitochondrion due to the high aﬃnity with negative MMP. After white light illumination, generated toxic ROS triggers cell death with reducing MMP and increasing nuclear permeability. Therefore, CDs nanozyme relocates to the nucleolus due to the binding with nucleic acids through electrostatic attraction. To further confirm the inference, carbonyl cyanide 3-chlorophenylhydrazone (CCCP) was used as a protonophore to decrease MMP. As indicated in Fig. S25, whether HeLa cells were incubated with CDs nanozyme and then treated with CCCP or pretreated with CCCP, both the nucleolus were occupied with CDs nanozyme, which was in accordance with the results of the irradiation-dependent staining assay of CDs nanozyme, validating the applicability of the proposed mechanism [42].

In summary, we have successfully synthesized the monocomponent multi-functional CDs nanozyme for inherent self-predicting treatment response in the process of cancer cell ablation triggered by itself. The CDs nanozyme was manufactured through a simple microwave-assisted strategy. The CDs nanozyme exhibits both OXD-like and POD-like activities under light irradiation, which could produce·O2 − in the presence of O2 and convert overproduced H2O2 in cancer cells into highly toxic·O2 −, consequently efficiently guiding cell death. In addition, the CDs nanozyme is distributed in mitochondrion initially, allowing for on­site and elevated therapy effect. More importantly, the CDs nanozyme undergoes mitochondrion-to-nucleolus transferring during cell apoptosis triggered by photo-responsive catalytic therapy, enabling the real-time sensing of treatment efficacy via ﬂuorescence signals translocation. A single platform has been constructed for simultaneous cancer cell ablation and self-predicting treatment efficacy that can help realize precise therapy, for example, by identifying drug efficacy and avoiding excessive or ineffective treatment. This work provides a successful paradigm for designing multi-functional CDs nanozyme and will promote the development of cancer theranostic studies.

Shuo Guo: Investigation, Material preparation, Writing original draft, Software, Yuanqiang Sun: Supervision, Review, Editing, Yue Xiao: Investigation, Material preparation, Lingbo Qu: Funding Acquisition, Supervision, Resources. Qiyong Cai: Material preparation, Review, Zhaohui Li: Funding Acquisition, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [21974125, 22274143], Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in the University of Henan Province (22TRTSTHN002), the 111 Project [D20003, CXJD2021001], and the Key Projects of the Joint Fund for Science and Technology of Henan Province (222301420007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.133977.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shuo Guo has been studying for her Ph.D. degree in the College of Chemistry at Zhengzhou University since 2020, where her main research interests are novel functional carbon dots based nanozymes for biosensor and bioimaging.

Yue Xiao received his PhD from the Institute of Chemical Biology and Nanomedicine of Hunan University in 2019. His research interests are the development of self-assembled polymer-based nanostructures for biosensing.
Yuanqiang Sun is an associate professor in the College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. His research interests focus on the synthesis of fluorescent probes and their biological applications.

Lingbo Qu received his PhD degree in Pharmaceutical Analysis at China Pharmaceutical University in 1997. His research interests mainly focus on the design of functional fluorescent nanomaterials and their biological applications.
Qiyong Cai received his PhD from the Institute of Chemical Biology and Nanomedicine of Hunan University. His research interests are the construction of single molecule system for biosensing.

Zhaohui Li is a professor of College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University. His research interests mainly focus on the development of nanomaterials synthesis and their bio-applications. He has published over 100 high-quality papers in related research fields.
